name: Fresno Unified School District
districtFact: Fresno Unified's Dual Language Immersion program in Hmong is one of the only K-12 Hmong bilingual education sequences in the United States — the Fresno metro is home to one of the largest Hmong communities in the country, and FUSD has invested in heritage-language instruction at a scale no other California district matches. Applications for the following school year typically close in December. Families relocating to Fresno who miss the DLI window must wait a full year before their child can enter the program.

about.title: About Fresno Unified School District
about.content:
Fresno Unified School District was founded in 1885 and today serves approximately 70,000 students across more than 100 schools — making it the fourth largest school district in California and the largest in the San Joaquin Valley. The district covers the City of Fresno proper, a city of roughly 550,000 that functions as the commercial and agricultural capital of Central California.

Fresno's demographic profile is unlike any other major California city. The San Joaquin Valley's agricultural economy brought generations of farmworker families — primarily from Mexico and Central America — whose children now make up the majority of FUSD's enrollment. Fresno is also home to one of the largest Hmong communities in the United States, a population that arrived largely as refugees from Laos following the Vietnam War. Approximately a hundred languages are spoken in FUSD schools; Spanish and Hmong are by far the most common home languages after English.

Approximately 85% of FUSD students qualify for free or reduced-price lunch — one of the highest rates of any large California school district and a reflection of the Valley's persistent poverty rate. The district's academic profile is also shaped by air quality: Fresno County ranks among the worst in the nation for ozone and particulate pollution, and wildfire smoke from the Sierra Nevada each fall adds acute AQI spikes on top of the chronic baseline. Schools adjust outdoor schedules on high-AQI days, and families with children who have asthma or respiratory conditions should understand the district's air quality protocols before the school year begins.

about.title: Programs and School Choice
about.content:
FUSD's most distinctive program is its Dual Language Immersion (DLI) sequence, offered in Spanish and Hmong from kindergarten through the upper grades. The Hmong DLI track is particularly rare — at the scale FUSD operates it, no other California district provides comparable K-12 Hmong bilingual instruction. Families with Hmong-speaking children relocating from other states should contact FUSD's DLI program office before arriving; applications for the following school year close in December, and a child who arrives after that window will be placed in a standard English-instruction class until the next enrollment cycle.

FUSD also operates one of California's most developed Agricultural Education programs, reflecting the San Joaquin Valley's economic identity. High school students can earn industry certifications in plant science, agriscience, food processing, and agricultural business through the Future Farmers of America (FFA) chapter network. FUSD's FFA chapters compete at both the state and national level; for students considering careers in agribusiness, food science, or environmental science in a region that produces roughly 25% of the US food supply, the Agricultural Education pathway carries genuine economic weight.

Career and Technical Education (CTE) pathways are available across multiple high school campuses, covering health careers, information technology, construction, and early childhood education. FUSD operates GATE (Gifted and Talented Education) testing and services at the elementary and middle school level; families with children who may qualify should request a GATE referral from the classroom teacher, as the district-administered assessment is not automatic.

FUSD high school athletics compete in the Central Section of the California Interscholastic Federation (CIF). The Central Section is one of the largest CIF sections by geographic area and is known for competitive football and basketball programs at several Fresno high schools. CIF playoff schedules run November through March for fall and winter sports and can extend the athletic season well beyond the end of the standard school quarter — families with student-athletes should follow their school's athletic department schedule separately from the district academic calendar.

Applications for DLI, GATE, and some CTE pathway programs involve separate timelines from standard enrollment. Contact the FUSD enrollment office at fresnounified.org to confirm current application dates before committing to a move date.

about.title: Before You Move to Fresno Unified
about.content:
Four things worth doing before committing to a Fresno address.

Verify your school assignment by exact address. FUSD attendance zones follow street-level boundaries that do not always correspond to neighborhood names or ZIP codes. The FUSD school locator at fresnounified.org uses your specific address to identify your assigned elementary, middle, and high school — confirm before signing a lease or mortgage.

Check the Dual Language Immersion application timeline if DLI placement matters. Applications for the following school year typically close in December. A family that arrives in January will not be able to enroll their child in DLI until the year after next — a potential 20-month gap. Families targeting Spanish or Hmong DLI should time their move to allow a fall application.

Add all Buyback Days to your personal calendar in August. Buyback Days are scattered no-school days for students that are published in the annual FUSD calendar but not always clearly marked in third-party calendar apps. New families are regularly caught off guard by them — add them to your calendar at the start of the year so childcare arrangements aren't last-minute.

Register for FUSD's parent notification system before the first day of school. Fresno's air quality and Tule fog conditions generate same-day school schedule changes — outdoor PE cancellations, recess modifications, and occasional late starts. The district announces these through its notification platform. Families with asthmatic children should also ensure rescue inhaler prescriptions are current before the mid-August start.

content: Set back-to-school reminders for mid-July — confirm enrollment paperwork, childcare, transportation, and supplies well before the mid-August first day.

Sign up for FUSD's parent notification system before the first day of school to receive same-day alerts for AQI changes, outdoor schedule adjustments, and emergency closures. If your child has asthma, ensure their rescue inhaler prescription is current before mid-August.
Update all school emergency contacts before November — Tule fog bus delays can appear with less than an hour's warning and are communicated through the district notification system.

Mark Lincoln's Birthday (~February 12) and Cesar Chavez Day (March 31) on your childcare calendar at the start of the school year — both are Fresno Unified no-school days not observed in most other states.
Add Buyback Days to your personal calendar in August when Fresno Unified publishes the final schedule — these student no-school days are scattered throughout the year and easy to miss.

Book spring break travel well before March. For Yosemite day visits, set a reminder to book exactly 28 days before your target date when the rolling reservation window opens. Check the Yosemite National Park website for current entry requirements before booking.
Contact Fresno Unified's school choice office by October if you're interested in Dual Language Immersion, CTE, or Agricultural Education programs — some applications close before December.

Families moving from coastal California are often surprised that air quality — not rain — is the most common reason outdoor PE is cancelled and recess moved indoors in Fresno. On high-AQI days from wildfire smoke or agricultural dust, outdoor activities are suspended before any weather event occurs. If your child has a respiratory condition, request an AQI action plan from the school nurse at enrollment.

Agricultural harvest cycles in the San Joaquin Valley run from July through October (stone fruit, grapes, almonds, citrus). Families whose livelihoods track harvest schedules — or who have extended family working seasonal agriculture — should note that harvest peaks overlap directly with the first weeks of school and the Thanksgiving period. Attendance patterns at many FUSD schools reflect this reality; the district has systems for managing family circumstances tied to seasonal agricultural work.

districtFaqs.q: What is a District Buyback Day in Fresno Unified?
districtFaqs.a: Buyback Days (also called Staff Development Days or Teacher Workdays) are days when teachers and staff report to school for professional development, training, or planning — but students do not attend. These are typically scattered throughout the school year and are listed in the official Fresno Unified calendar. The dates are finalized and published each August — add them to your personal calendar as soon as the annual calendar is released, as third-party calendar apps do not always display Buyback Days clearly. New families are regularly caught off guard by them.

districtFaqs.q: Is Fresno Unified the same as Fresno State?
districtFaqs.a: No. Fresno Unified School District (FUSD) is the K-12 public school system serving the city of Fresno. California State University, Fresno (Fresno State) is a separate university with its own academic calendar. Fresno State's semester schedule does not affect Fresno Unified school dates.

districtFaqs.q: Why is Lincoln's Birthday a holiday in Fresno Unified?
districtFaqs.a: California state law designates Abraham Lincoln's Birthday (February 12) as a paid holiday for school employees. Not all California districts close schools on this date, but many Central Valley districts, including Fresno Unified, observe it as a separate no-school day. This holiday is specific to California and is not recognized in most other states — families relocating from outside California are often surprised to find two no-school Mondays in February when Lincoln's Birthday and Presidents' Day fall in the same month.

districtFaqs.q: Why is Cesar Chavez Day a school holiday in Fresno Unified?
districtFaqs.a: Cesar Chavez Day (March 31) is a California state holiday honoring the civil rights and labor activist who co-founded the United Farm Workers union. In Fresno Unified, it is observed as a no-school day for both students and staff. The holiday has particular resonance in the San Joaquin Valley, where the farmworker movement Chavez led shaped the economic and cultural history of the region. Families relocating from outside California should note that this holiday is not observed in most other states.

districtFaqs.q: Does Fresno Unified cancel school for bad air quality or wildfire smoke?
districtFaqs.a: Fresno Unified does not typically cancel school for air quality alone, but the district modifies outdoor activities when AQI levels are elevated. On high-AQI days, outdoor PE may be moved indoors, recess may be shortened or held inside, and after-school athletic practices may be adjusted. During severe wildfire smoke events, the district may change dismissal procedures or close schools entirely. Fresno County has some of the worst air quality in California — families with children who have asthma, allergies, or respiratory conditions should register for FUSD's parent notification system to receive same-day schedule change alerts and check AirNow.gov before morning drop-off during fire season (typically August through November).

districtFaqs.q: Does Fresno Unified have delays or closures for Tule fog?
districtFaqs.a: Yes. Dense Tule fog in the San Joaquin Valley — typically from November through February — can reduce visibility to near zero and trigger school bus delays or late-start schedules. Unlike most of California, Fresno does not face snow-day closures, but fog is the weather event that Fresno families plan around each winter. FUSD communicates fog-related schedule changes through its parent notification system — register before November so you receive alerts when they are announced, which can be as late as 5 or 6 AM on fog-affected mornings.

districtFaqs.q: Does Fresno Unified have a Dual Language Immersion program, and how do I apply?
districtFaqs.a: Yes. Fresno Unified operates Dual Language Immersion programs in Spanish and Hmong, beginning in kindergarten. The Hmong DLI program is among the most developed K-12 Hmong bilingual sequences in the United States, reflecting Fresno's large Hmong community. Applications for DLI enrollment typically open in fall and close in December for the following school year. Families who arrive in Fresno after the December deadline will not be able to enroll their child in DLI until the next application cycle — potentially a 20-month delay. Contact FUSD's enrollment office at fresnounified.org well before your intended move date to confirm current application timelines.

districtFaqs.q: Does Fresno Unified have a GATE program for gifted students?
districtFaqs.a: Yes. Fresno Unified offers GATE (Gifted and Talented Education) services at the elementary and middle school level, consistent with California Education Code. GATE identification requires a formal referral and district-administered assessment — it is not automatic. Families who believe their child may qualify should request a GATE referral from the classroom teacher. The assessment window and eligibility criteria vary by school year; contact your assigned school or FUSD's student services department at fresnounified.org for current testing timelines.

districtFaqs.q: When does kindergarten registration open for Fresno Unified?
districtFaqs.a: Fresno Unified kindergarten registration typically opens in January or February for the following fall school year. California requires children to be five years old by September 1 to enter kindergarten. Required documents typically include: certified birth certificate, current California immunization record, and proof of Fresno Unified residency (utility bill, lease, or mortgage statement). Families relocating to Fresno in late spring or summer should contact their assigned neighborhood school directly rather than waiting for a formal registration event, as seats at some campuses fill early.

districtFaqs.q: What are typical school hours for Fresno Unified campuses?
districtFaqs.a: Fresno Unified school hours vary by campus and grade level. Elementary schools generally start between 7:45 and 8:15 AM and dismiss between 2:15 and 3:15 PM. Middle and high school schedules differ by campus. FUSD does not operate a uniform bell schedule across all schools — confirm your assigned school's specific start and dismissal times at fresnounified.org before the year begins. Bell schedules are also used to determine bus eligibility, so verifying start times matters for families relying on FUSD transportation.

districtFaqs.q: Does Fresno Unified have Fall Break?
districtFaqs.a: No. Fresno Unified does not have a Fall Break. The school year from August through Thanksgiving runs without a multi-day break for students — the only no-school days between Labor Day and Thanksgiving are District Buyback Days, Veterans Day, and any district-specific observances published in the annual calendar. Families relocating from districts that observe a Fall Break in October should adjust their autumn childcare and travel planning accordingly.

districtFaqs.q: Why is Fresno Unified's Winter Break so long compared to other California districts?
districtFaqs.a: Fresno Unified's Winter Break typically runs from late December through the first week of January — longer than the standard two-week break at most California districts. The extended break reflects a district calendar structure that concentrates time off during winter rather than distributing additional breaks across fall. The exact start and end dates change each school year; check the official FUSD calendar each August for the confirmed Winter Break window and plan childcare arrangements accordingly.

districtFaqs.q: How do I verify which Fresno Unified school my child will attend?
districtFaqs.a: Use the school locator tool at fresnounified.org, which uses your exact street address to identify your assigned elementary, middle, and high school. FUSD attendance zone boundaries run at the street level — two homes in the same neighborhood or ZIP code can be assigned to different schools if a boundary runs between them. Always verify school assignment by your specific address before committing to a lease or home purchase, not by neighborhood name or proximity to a school.

districtFaqs.q: Does Fresno Unified provide school bus transportation?
districtFaqs.a: Fresno Unified provides bus transportation to eligible students based on distance from the assigned school. Eligibility criteria and bus routes are published at fresnounified.org/transportation. Families should confirm bus availability for their specific address and school assignment before the school year begins — not all areas of Fresno are served by the same routes, and eligibility can vary by grade level and campus.

districtFaqs.q: Why does Fresno Unified start school in mid-August when most California districts start later?
districtFaqs.a: Fresno Unified typically begins the school year in mid-August — earlier than most coastal California districts, which often start after Labor Day or in late August. The earlier start allows FUSD to conclude the school year in early June before Central Valley temperatures reach their peak (100°F+ heat is common in June and July in Fresno). Starting in August also gives the district the instructional days it needs while ending before the extreme summer heat makes school-day operations more difficult for facilities without full air conditioning capacity.

livingHere.intro: Fresno sits at the geographic center of California's San Joaquin Valley, surrounded by the most productive agricultural land in the United States. For families relocating here, the city's rhythms are shaped less by coastal trends and more by the land, climate, and economy that define Central Valley life.

highlights.label: Extreme Summer Heat
highlights.detail: Fresno regularly records temperatures above 105°F from June through August — among the hottest of any major California city. Air conditioning is essential, and outdoor afternoon activities during back-to-school weeks in late July are genuinely hazardous for young children.

highlights.label: Tule Fog Season
highlights.detail: Dense Tule fog rolls through the San Joaquin Valley from November through February, frequently reducing visibility to near zero. School bus delays and closures are common during fog advisories — fog, not snow, is the weather event Fresno families plan around each winter.

highlights.label: Agricultural Employment Cycles
highlights.detail: The Fresno economy is deeply tied to grape, citrus, almond, and stone fruit harvests. Seasonal agricultural employment peaks in summer and fall, affecting work schedules and family availability in ways that do not apply to urban districts elsewhere in California.

highlights.label: Yosemite National Park Access
highlights.detail: Yosemite's South Entrance (Wawona/Mariposa Grove) is approximately 65 miles from Fresno — roughly a 90-minute drive. Summer reservations for Yosemite Valley day visits sell out within minutes of release, often in January or February. Plan school-year break trips to Yosemite during spring or fall for better access.

highlights.label: Fresno Yosemite International Airport (FAT)
highlights.detail: FAT is a small regional airport with limited direct routes — mostly to West Coast hubs, Phoenix, and Las Vegas. Nonstop options are significantly fewer than at LAX or SFO. Most long-distance travel requires a connecting flight or a 3-hour drive to the Bay Area.

highlights.label: Air Quality and Valley Fever
highlights.detail: Fresno County has among the worst air quality in the United States, driven by agricultural dust, vehicle emissions, and wildfire smoke each fall. On high-AQI days, Fresno Unified may move outdoor PE classes indoors and shorten recess — families should enable school notifications and check AirNow before morning drop-off during fire season. Valley Fever (a fungal lung infection from soil dust) is endemic to the San Joaquin Valley; families new to the region should discuss it with their pediatrician, especially for children who spend time outdoors.

sources.label: Fresno Unified School District official calendar
Fresno Unified School District official website
California Department of Education district data
Yosemite National Park — recreation and reservations

compareIntro: FUSD's school calendar is shaped by geography, not statewide convention. The mid-August start exists because Central Valley temperatures reach 105°F+ by late June, and ending before that heat peak is a practical necessity — not a choice. Most California coastal districts start in late August or after Labor Day because they don't face the same summer heat. FUSD's 8-day Spring Break is also structurally longer than the 5-day standard at most California districts, a pattern that reflects how the district builds its 180-day calendar around the agricultural labor cycle and winter break length, not coastline customs. Families moving to Fresno from LA, the Bay Area, or Sacramento should expect a meaningfully different calendar rhythm, not a minor date shift.

relatedDistricts.name: Sacramento City Unified School District
relatedDistricts.comparisonNote: Sacramento City Unified sits 160 miles north in the Sacramento Valley, where summer temperatures — while hot — are moderated by the Sacramento River delta and cooler maritime air from the Bay. SCUSD does not face the same pressure to start before Labor Day that drives FUSD's mid-August calendar. Sacramento typically starts school in late August and ends in early June on a tighter spring break of 5 days, compared to FUSD's 8-day window. Both districts serve high-poverty urban populations with large Latino communities, but their linguistic profiles differ — Fresno's Hmong population is one of the largest in the US and shapes FUSD's Dual Language Immersion offerings in a way that has no equivalent in Sacramento City schools. Families relocating along the CA-99 corridor between the two cities should not assume calendar alignment; the two districts have structurally different school-year builds.

relatedDistricts.name: Long Beach Unified School District
relatedDistricts.comparisonNote: Long Beach Unified operates under an entirely different climate and economic context than Fresno Unified. Long Beach's marine layer keeps summer temperatures mild — rarely above 85°F — so LBUSD faces no pressure to front-load the school year before summer heat. LBUSD typically starts in late August, runs a standard 5-day Spring Break, and does not deal with Tule fog, wildfire smoke advisories, or AQI-driven outdoor cancellations that are routine operational realities in Fresno. Long Beach also has a large Cambodian-American community — the largest in the US — paralleling Fresno's Hmong community in some ways but without a comparable bilingual DLI sequence at the district scale FUSD operates. Spring break windows between LBUSD and FUSD frequently fall in different weeks. Families transferring between the LA metro and the Central Valley are moving between two genuinely different school environments, not two versions of the same California experience.
